evidence: R1 running-config (get_running_config): Serial0/1/0 has ip access-group R1_IN in (access-group applied inbound).
R1 running-config (get_running_config): ACL 'R1_IN' sequence 10 = deny icmp 172.16.0.0 0.0.0.255 any echo (blocks ICMP echo from 172.16.0.0/24).
R2 OSPF (get_ospf): R2 reports Serial0/1/0 neighbor 1.1.1.1 at 10.0.0.1 (ospf-nbr-full) â€” indicates routing adjacency between R1 and R2 is up.
R2 interface status (get_interface_status): GigabitEthernet0/0/0 admin_state = up, oper_state = up, input_errors = 0, output_errors = 0 (R2 LAN interface is up).
remaining_issues: R1 is configured to block ICMP echo (ICMP type=echo) sourced from 172.16.0.0/24 via ACL 'R1_IN' applied inbound on the R1<->R2 serial link. This will block ICMP echo-replies from 172.16.0.11 back to 192.168.0.11 and is the likely cause of the failed pings.
Until the ACL is adjusted or removed, pings from 192.168.0.11 to 172.16.0.11 will continue to fail even though OSPF adjacency and R2 LAN interface are up.

Recommended immediate remediation: update 'R1_IN' to permit ICMP (or remove the specific deny), or apply a more specific permit for return traffic. After change, run verification (ping/traceroute from R1 and/or the host) to confirm reachability.

Post-remediation verification steps to perform: from R1 run 'ping 172.16.0.11' and 'traceroute 172.16.0.11'; on R2 run 'show ip arp 172.16.0.11' and 'show interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0'; optionally ping the host from R2 to confirm host responsiveness.

messages.content:
What I observed
- Report: 192.168.0.11 â†’ 172.16.0.11 pings fail (0/25).  
- From router checks: R1 and R2 can both ping 172.16.0.11. R1 can ping 192.168.0.11. Gi0/0/0 on R1 is up with no errors. OSPF adjacencies are healthy.

Diagnosis (root cause)
- Return ICMP is being dropped at R1: R1 has an inbound ACL named R1_IN applied to Serial0/1/0 that contains an explicit "deny icmp 172.16.0.0/24" entry. That will block ICMP replies from 172.16.0.11 returning through R2â†’R1 to the 192.168.0.0/24 LAN, explaining host-to-host ping failure.

Changes made
- No configuration changes were made. I collected running-configs, interface status, OSPF state, and ran pings/traceroutes from R1 and R2.

Verification â€” passed/failed
- Passed: R1 and R2 can each reach 172.16.0.11 (pings 5/5). R1 can reach 192.168.0.11. Interfaces are up and show no errors.
- Failed: end-to-end host-to-host connectivity remains unresolved (original problem persists). Evidence: host-level pings were not available from the endpoint; traceroutes from R1 to 192.168.0.11 returned no TTL replies (anomaly) but pings succeeded â€” ACL behavior matches observed symptom.

What remains / recommended next steps
1. Confirm ACL hits: run "show ip access-lists" on R1 and R2 to get hit counters for R1_IN, R1_TO_R2, R2_TO_R1.  
2. On R1, verify host ARP and LAN interface: "show ip arp 192.168.0.11" and "show interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0".  
3. From the endpoint 192.168.0.11, provide ping (25 packets) and traceroute to 172.16.0.11 to confirm current behavior and timestamps.  
4. Remediation option (if ACL hit counters confirm drops): remove or change the R1_IN deny-icmp rule (or narrow it) so ICMP replies from 172.16.0.0/24 are permitted, then re-test host-to-host pings.

If you want, I can immediately collect the ACL counters and ARP/interface info and then either patch the ACL or apply a temporary allow for ICMP and re-test.
